and without being influenced by any other consideration, it cannot be 
said that there was vitiation of consent under misconception of fact. 
It was further held that even if it is assumed that a false promise of 
marriage was made to the complainant initially by the accused, the 
fact that the relationship continued for a period of nine long years 
would render the plea of the complainant that her consent for all 
these years was under misconception of the fact that the accused 
would marry her implausible.
28. In the case of Prashant v. State (NCT of Delhi),7 this Court observed 
that it is inconceivable that the complainant would continue to meet the 
accused or maintain a prolonged association or physical relationship 
with him in the absence of voluntary consent on her part. 
29. Testing the facts of the case at hand, on the touchstone of the above 
precedents, it is clear that the complainant, being a highly qualified
